text: The surgeon visually confirms the tumor location at the ascending colon during the laparoscopic procedure.
The surgeon begins the procedure by identifying and isolating the ileocolic vessels, preparing for their ligation and division.
The surgeon performs mobilization of the right mesocolon using a medial to lateral approach, carefully separating the mesocolon from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon divides the ileocolic vessels after applying clips, ensuring secure vessel ligation during the procedure.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the hepatic flexure and the proximal transverse colon, detaching these sections from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the transverse colon by dividing the coloepiploid ligament, separating it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon approaches the left mesocolon from the medial side, continuing the dissection and mobilization process.
The surgeon identifies and isolates the inferior mesenteric artery and vein, carefully separating these vessels from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon divides the inferior mesenteric artery and the middle colic vessels following the application of surgical clips, ensuring secure vessel ligation.
The surgeon performs an endoscopic transection of the terminal ileum, using precise instruments to separate the ileum from adjacent structures.
The surgeon uses a zero polypropylene suture to perform a per-string closure of the rectal lumen, securing the tissue in place.
The surgeon marks the rectal mucosa circumferentially distal to the per-strain closure, followed by performing a rectotomy approximately four centimeters above the dentate line.
The surgeon conducts mesorectal dissection, starting with the posterior and anterior planes, which are visibly easier to identify.
The surgeon continues the dissection on the lateral sides, carefully navigating around the neurovascular bundles.
The surgeon mobilizes the mesorectal plane using a circumferential progressive pattern, carefully separating the tissue layers around the rectum.
The surgeon continues the dissection until reaching the Douglas pouch, progressing through the mesorectal plane with visible tissue separation.
The surgeon creates the J pouch using two firings of a 100mm linear stapler, shaping the tissue into the desired configuration.
The surgeon verifies the vascularization of the J pouch using real-time angiography, following the injection of indocyanine green, which highlights the blood flow within the tissue.
The surgeon dissects the mesentery of the J pouch, carefully freeing it from the duodenum to maximize its reach.

The surgeon approximates the J pouch to the pelvis, preparing for anastomosis. A purse string is applied at the distal resection margin, followed by the creation of the anastomosis using a 31mm circular stapler.
